title: Mola-inspired layered papercut
subtitle: Stacking coloured tissue layers and cutting away the top layers to reveal the colours beneath, a paper interpretation of Kuna reverse appliqué
excerpt: Kuna mola textiles are built by stacking layers of coloured fabric and cutting away the top layers to reveal the colours beneath. The same logic translates directly to paper: stack three or four sheets of different-coloured tissue, draw a bold geometric design on the top sheet, and cut away areas of the upper layers with a craft knife to expose the layers underneath. The finished work is thin, light, and shows colour depth that single-layer cutting cannot achieve.
sourceNotes: Mola textile tradition of the Kuna (Guna) people of Panama and Colombia; the paper adaptation is a contemporary craft interpretation documented widely in paper arts literature. No single canonical source for the paper version.

glossaryTerms.definition: A multi-layer reverse-appliqué textile panel made by the Kuna people of Panama and Colombia. Layers of differently coloured cloth are stacked and cut through from the top downwards, then the cut edges are folded back and hemmed to reveal the layers beneath. The technique produces bold geometric and figurative designs with visible colour depth.

A cutting technique in which areas of the top layer are removed to reveal a contrasting colour or pattern beneath. The design is defined by what is cut away rather than what remains, the opposite logic of silhouette cutting.

The accurate alignment of successive paper layers so that design elements from lower layers appear exactly where intended relative to the cuts in upper layers. In a three-layer cut, misregistration of even 2 mm shifts all the colour relationships.

content.text: A layered papercut works like a

: you cut down through the stack rather than across a flat plane. The design reveals itself as each layer of the stack is cut. Tissue paper is the right weight, three or four sheets stack to less than a millimetre and the craft knife cuts through one layer at a time with control.

items.name: Light box, A4 (for tracing design through layers)

content.text: Plan the design as three concentric zones, each corresponding to one layer of the stack. The outermost zone is the top layer (the most visible colour); the middle zone appears where the top layer is cut away; the innermost zone is the bottom layer. Draw the design at A5 size on paper first, bold geometric shapes with no elements thinner than 5 mm work best. Fine details are lost in tissue paper's slight transparency.
Stack the tissue sheets in colour order: bottom colour first, top colour last. Tape all four corners to maintain

Transfer the design to the top layer with light pencil marks. Work on the light box if available, the transmitted light makes all layers visible simultaneously, helping you track where each cut lands in the colour sequence.

areas: cut through the top layer only, using the scalpel for curved lines and the craft knife against a ruler for straight edges. Remove the cut sections from the top layer only, leaving the layers beneath intact. Then, where you want to reveal the bottom colour, cut through the top two layers together.

items.symptom: The craft knife cuts through all layers instead of just the top one
items.cause: Blade too sharp for the light tissue, or too much downward pressure.
items.fix: Use a slightly worn blade for tissue-layer control, a very sharp blade needs almost no pressure and cuts through multiple layers unexpectedly. Alternatively, work with a single layer at a time: remove the top layer, cut it separately, then re-stack.

items.symptom: The layers shift during cutting, misaligning the design
items.fix: Add tape at the midpoints of each edge as well as the corners. Work from the centre of the design outward rather than from one side to the other.
